title: The Third Level by Jack Finney
subject: English

notes.heading: Theme
notes.text: Escapism, nostalgia for a simpler past, and the harshness of modern life. The third level is a metaphor for the human desire to escape stress and insecurity.

summary:
The story is about Charley, a 31-year-old man who claims to have discovered a third level at Grand Central Station in New York. Grand Central officially has only two levels, but one night while hurrying to catch a train home, Charley gets lost and finds himself on a third level that looks like the 1890s.

On the third level, everything is old-fashioned. People wear derby hats and four-button suits. There are gas lights, brass spittoons, and an open-flame furnace. The newspaper is The World, dated June 11, 1894. Charley realizes that he has gone back in time. He is fascinated because 1894 seems to be a peaceful era before world wars and modern stress.

Charley wants to buy two tickets to Galesburg, Illinois, his grandfather’s hometown, described as a quiet, simple place with big houses, huge lawns, and summer evenings. But the clerk gets suspicious when Charley tries to pay with modern money. Charley runs away to avoid arrest.

When Charley tells his psychiatrist friend Sam about it, Sam says it is a waking-dream wish fulfilment. Charley is unhappy with modern insecurity and wants to escape. Even Charley’s wife Louisa thinks he is imagining it. But Charley keeps looking for the third level and exchanges his money for old-style bills.

One day, Charley finds a first-day cover mailed to his grandfather in Galesburg, but it is addressed to Charley. Inside is a letter from Sam. Sam says he found the third level, is living in Galesburg of 1894, and invites Charley and Louisa to join him. Ironically, the man who called Charley’s escape wish fulfilment has escaped himself. Charley keeps searching for the third level, hoping to reach peaceful Galesburg.

qa.q: What does the third level refer to?
qa.a: The third level refers to a mysterious extra level at Grand Central Station. Charley believes it is a passage to the year 1894, a peaceful world of the past. His psychiatrist says it is only a waking-dream wish fulfilment caused by Charley’s desire to escape from the stress of modern life.

qa.q: Would Charley ever go back to the ticket-counter on the third level to buy tickets to Galesburg for himself and his wife?
qa.a: Yes, Charley wanted to go back to the third level and buy tickets to Galesburg for himself and Louisa. He even bought old-style currency for this purpose. But he could not find the third level again, although he searched for it many times. Later, Sam’s letter encouraged him and Louisa to keep looking for it.

qa.q: Do you think that the third level was a medium of escape for Charley? Why?
qa.a: Yes, the third level was a medium of escape for Charley. He was living in a modern world full of insecurity, fear, war, worry and stress. The third level gave him a way to escape into 1894, a time he imagined as peaceful, simple and happy.

qa.q: What do you infer from Sam’s letter to Charley?
qa.a: Sam’s letter shows that he too found the third level and escaped to Galesburg in 1894. He was happy there and invited Charley and Louisa to come. This suggests that Sam also wanted to escape from modern life and live in a simpler world.

qa.q: ‘The modern world is full of insecurity, fear, war, worry and stress.’ What are the ways in which we attempt to overcome them?
qa.a: People try to overcome insecurity and stress in different ways. Some take up hobbies like stamp collecting, reading, music, travel, meditation, sports, or spending time with family. Others escape through imagination, memories, dreams, films, or stories. Charley used stamp collecting and the idea of the third level as a refuge from reality.

qa.q: Do you see an intersection of time and space in the story?
qa.a: Yes, the story shows an intersection of time and space. Grand Central Station is a real place in New York, but its third level becomes a passage to another time, the year 1894. Thus, the story connects the present world with the past through one physical place.

qa.q: Apparent illogicality sometimes turns out to be a futuristic projection. Discuss.
qa.a: Yes, what appears illogical today may become possible in the future. In the story, time travel through the third level seems impossible, but it reflects the human desire to move beyond the limits of time and space. Many ideas that once seemed impossible, like flying, space travel, or instant communication, later became real. So the story’s fantasy can be seen as a futuristic projection.
